4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Dean Pugh on UnsplashIn Last Blog , we understood why Object-Oriented Programming exists.
Today, we’ll learn the two most fundamental concepts in OOP:

• Class

• Object
Every Low-Level Design problem — whether it’s Uber, BookMyShow, Splitwise, or a Parking Lot — starts by identifying the right classes and creating objects from them.
Let’s keep it simple and interview-focused.

What Is a Class?
A class is a blueprint or template that defines the state (attributes) and behavior (methods) of objects.
Think of it as an architectural blueprint for a house.
The blueprint tells you:

• How many rooms the house has

• Where the doors are

• Where the kitchen is
But you can’t live in a blueprint.
Similarly, a class only defines what an object should look like — it doesn’t represent a real entity.
Example:
class Car {
String brand;
String model;
int speed;
void start() {}
void accelerate() {}
}The Car class defines what every car should have.

What Is an Object?
An object is a runtime instance of a class with its own independent state and behavior.
If the class is the blueprint,
the object is the actual house built using that blueprint.
Example:
Car bmw = new Car();
Car audi = new Car();Here:

• bmw is an object.

• audi is another object.
Both are created from the same Car class, but each stores its own data.

State vs Behavior
Every object has two characteristics.
State
State is the data stored inside an object.
For a Car:
brand = BMW
model = M5
speed = 80These values can change during the object’s lifetime.

Behavior
Behavior is what the object can do.
For a Car:
start()
accelerate()
brake()
stop()Methods define an object’s behavior.

Class vs Object
ClassObjectBlueprint or templateRuntime instance of a classDefines structureHolds actual dataDoesn’t occupy memory for dataOccupies memory when createdCreated onceCan have multiple instances
Example:
class Car { }Car bmw = new Car();
Car audi = new Car();
Car tesla = new Car();One class.
Three independent objects.

What Is an Instance?
An instance is simply another name for an object.
When we write:
Car bmw = new Car();We say:
“bmw is an instance of the Car class."
Interviewers use both terms interchangeably.

What Does the new Keyword Do?
When Java executes:
Car car = new Car();The new keyword:

• Allocates memory for the object.

• Calls the constructor.

• Returns a reference to the newly created object.
Without new, no object is created.

What Is a Constructor?
A constructor is a special method that initializes an object when it is created.
Example:
class Car {
String brand;
Car(String brand) {
this.brand = brand;
}
}Usage:
Car bmw = new Car("BMW");Instead of creating an empty object and assigning values later, the object is initialized immediately.

What Does this Mean?
this refers to the current object.
Example:
class Car {
String brand;
Car(String brand) {
this.brand = brand;
}
}Here:
this.brandmeans
“The brand field of the current object."
It distinguishes the object’s field from the constructor parameter.

Why This Matters in LLD
Suppose the interviewer asks:
Design a Library Management System.
Before writing a single line of code, you should identify the real-world entities.
Possible classes:

• Library

• Book

• Member

• Librarian

• Loan

• Shelf
During runtime, these become objects.
Book #1
Book #2
Member Alice
Member Bob
Loan #101This is the first step of every Low-Level Design interview.

Interview Definitions
Class
A blueprint or template that defines the state and behavior of objects.
Object
A runtime instance of a class with its own independent state and behavior.
State
The data stored inside an object.
Behavior
The operations an object can perform.
Instance
Another term for an object created from a class.
Constructor
A special method used to initialize an object during creation.
this
A reference to the current object.

Key Takeaways

• A class defines what an object should look like.

• An object is a real instance created from a class.

• Every object has state (data) and behavior (methods).

• A constructor initializes an object.

• The new keyword creates an object in memory.

• this refers to the current object.
These concepts may seem basic, but they’re the foundation of every Low-Level Design interview. Every entity you model — whether it’s a User, Booking, Payment, or Vehicle—will eventually become a class, and every real entity in your running application will be an object.
